title: Chapter 4 (intermediate) — Destructible terrain & shared state
meta.desc: Intermediate level: the heightfield and craters, seed-based determinism, point solidity by squared distance, the compact snapshot, and a preview of turbo's continuous wind.
hero.title: Destructible terrain & shared state
hero.lead: How a battlefield can be torn apart and still stay perfectly in sync across screens: a heightfield + craters, seed-based determinism, a compact snapshot, and a first look at turbo's continuous wind.
s1.h2: The terrain is a heightfield, not an image
s1.lead: The destructible ground isn't a big bitmap. It's a heightfield: one surface height per column, plus a growing list of craters. Cheap to store, cheap to send, and easy to keep identical everywhere.
s1.code.cap: src/sim/terrain.js — the surface height per column
s1.code.c2: // sum of sine waves → natural relief
s1.p1: Destruction doesn't rewrite that array. Instead, we keep a list of craters alongside it. The terrain's final shape is “the heightfield minus the craters” — a subtraction computed on demand, never materialised.
s1.callout.tip: State = heights + craters. The whole battlefield is two pieces of data. Everything the game needs to ask — “is this point solid?”, “did the ball land?” — derives from them.
s2.p1: A point is solid when it sits below the surface and inside no crater. Testing “inside a crater?” is a circle test — and we do it without a square root by comparing the squared distance to the squared radius.
s2.p2: That tiny rule is enough to produce caves and overhangs: a crater dug under a bump leaves the matter above it floating, because that matter is still below the surface and inside no crater.
s2.callout.nerd: For the curious. Comparing dx²+dy² with c.r² is the trick from the maths annex: the square root is monotonic, so for a yes/no test the squares answer the same question — without the expensive root.
s2.xsec.title: Solid minus craters
s2.xsec.cap: Solid = below the surface minus the craters. A crater below the surface can leave material above it: that's how caves and overhangs appear.
s3.h2: Determinism: send the seed, not the terrain
s3.p1: The terrain is a pure function of a seed. The server rolls one number, generates the heights, and ships only that seed in the snapshot. Each client regenerates the identical surface — so the ground the server resolves collisions against is the very ground the TV draws.
s3.p2: And the craters? Just a short list of { x, y, r } circles. So the entire destructible state on the wire is one seed + a handful of crater circles — not a heightfield, never a bitmap.
s3.callout.tip: Shared state, kept small. Sending the whole terrain ~30 times a second would be wasteful and could desync. Sending the recipe (seed) plus the diffs (craters) is both lighter and self-consistent.
s4.h2: A taste of turbo: continuous wind
s4.p1: Classic mode rolls a fresh wind each turn. Turbo mode keeps the wind flowing: it eases between random keypoints every 10 seconds with a smoothstep curve, and layers a light gust wave on top so the breeze breathes.
s4.p2: On top, a light gust wave (two sines) rides over the base, its amplitude set by a per-match trait (gustiness). The result: a wind that drifts gently between keypoints but breathes instead of gliding mechanically. The expert level details the full formula.
s4.callout.nerd: Remember. Batch 4's architecture is about shared state that stays small and deterministic: terrain as heights + craters, solidity by squared distance, the seed as the recipe — and wind that flows continuously when the pace heats up. The full maths is in the maths annex.
